diagnosis.score_summary: With an overall alignment of 33/100, the brand operates with two fundamentally different personalities. The website projects a serious, clinical authority, while the Amazon presence is a warm, trend-aware guide. This deep schizophrenia across 12 critical markers actively erodes brand equity and confuses customers.

truth_1.headline: The Deity vs. The Guide
truth_1.gap_description: On its website, the brand acts as 'The Reliable Expert,' an emotionally distant but supremely competent authority. On Amazon, it transforms into 'The Trendsetter,' an affectionate and helpful guide. You are a cold, unquestionable god in your own temple and a warm, friendly advisor in the marketplace.
truth_1.evidence: [PERSONALITY] WWW: 'The Reliable Expert' — 'Korea’s No.1 Beauty Device Brand' vs. PDP: 'The Trendsetter' — 'PRO BEAUTY TIP, Mix!'
truth_1.why_matters: This personality split creates massive cognitive dissonance. Customers seeking the authoritative 'Deity' from the website will be confused by the chatty 'Guide' on Amazon, and vice-versa. This erodes trust in the brand's core identity and makes the premium positioning feel inauthentic.
truth_1.the_hard_choice: The brand is caught between being respected and being liked. You must decide if your equity comes from clinical authority or from approachable expertise. Option A: Erase the 'PRO BEAUTY TIP' warmth from Amazon and enforce the distant, authoritative 'Reliable Expert' voice everywhere to protect your premium positioning. Option B: Concede that the marketplace prefers warmth and rewrite the website to be a helpful 'Trendsetter,' sacrificing the 'Deity' status for higher engagement. A god does not give tips, and a guide does not rule from on high.

truth_2.headline: The Majority vs. The In-Crowd
truth_2.gap_description: The website promises social safety by positioning the brand as 'The Safe Choice'—the #1 brand everyone uses. The PDP promises social status by framing it as entry into 'The Knowledge Club'—a smart, niche choice for skincare insiders. You're selling safety in numbers on one channel and elite knowledge on another.
truth_2.evidence: [SOCIAL BENEFITS] WWW: 'The Safe Choice' — 'Korea’s No.1 Beauty Device Brand' vs. PDP: 'The Knowledge Club' — 'PRO BEAUTY TIP, Blend & Glow!'
truth_2.why_matters: This conflict confuses the target audience. Are you for everyone, or are you for the discerning few? Trying to be both cheapens the 'insider' appeal and makes the 'majority' claim feel like a commodity play. You lose the ability to build a cohesive community.
truth_2.the_hard_choice: You are telling two different stories about who your customer is, and you must pick a tribe. Option A: Lean into the mass-market 'Safe Choice' story by stripping all niche, 'insider' language from Amazon and focusing purely on your '#1 in Korea' status. Option B: Acknowledge that the Amazon 'Knowledge Club' is where the energy is, and rewrite your website to celebrate the sophisticated consumer, abandoning the generic 'majority' claim. You cannot be both the default choice and the secret handshake.

truth_3.headline: Investment vs. Indulgence
truth_3.gap_description: The website positions the brand as 'The Scientific Miracle,' a serious, professional investment in skin technology. The PDP positions it as 'The Clean Essentialist,' a polished, affordable indulgence for the trend-aware. The brand is high-tech lab equipment on one channel and a 'shelfie-ready' accessory on the other.
truth_3.evidence: [POSITIONING] WWW: 'The Scientific Miracle' — 'professional-grade skincare' vs. PDP: 'The Clean Essentialist' — 'Plump Up Your Glow!'
truth_3.why_matters: This positioning clash directly undermines your pricing power. A customer who sees the trendy, affordable 'Indulgence' on Amazon will question the premium 'Investment' price on the website. You are training your audience to see you as a cheap thrill, not a long-term solution.
truth_3.the_hard_choice: Your brand is straddling two different price universes, and this ambiguity will always resolve downwards. You must choose your financial identity. Option A: Discipline the PDP to reflect the premium, 'Scientific Miracle' positioning, using more clinical language, even if it hurts short-term conversion. Option B: Embrace the high-volume 'Clean Essentialist' reality, lower your website's perceived value to match the PDP, and compete on accessibility and trendiness. You can't charge for a miracle and deliver a daily indulgence.

truth_4.headline: The Unifying Visual Soul
truth_4.gap_description: Despite catastrophic gaps in personality and positioning, the brand's visual identity is perfectly consistent. Both the website and PDP execute the 'Earthy Organic' territory, using a warm, radiating color palette (soft pinks) and a clean, structured layout. This is the single thread holding the two brand personalities together.
truth_4.evidence: [VISUAL IDENTITY] Both channels execute 'Earthy Organic' with a warm palette and structured layout. WWW: 'palette_color_1: #FCE8E7', PDP: 'palette_color_1: #F8E0E4'
truth_4.why_matters: This visual consistency is the brand's saving grace. It provides a subconscious anchor of recognition for the customer, proving that a unified brand is possible. It's the foundation upon which a consistent narrative can be rebuilt, ensuring that no matter how confusing the words are, the product looks like it came from the same company.
truth_4.the_hard_choice: The hard choice here is not about choosing a path, but about recognizing what works. Your visual system is the most disciplined part of your brand, successfully making clinical science feel approachable. Option A: Treat this as a happy accident. Option B: Explicitly name this 'Warm Science' aesthetic as a core brand pillar and force the copy, tone, and personality to align with the visuals that are already working. Your pictures are telling a better story than your words; it's time to let them lead.

truth_5.headline: Transformation vs. Enhancement
truth_5.gap_description: Both channels correctly identify the brand's purpose as 'The Empowerer.' However, the website makes a much bolder promise of deep psychological 'Transformation' (5/5), while the PDP offers a more modest aesthetic 'Enhancement' (4/5). The brand's home base has a more ambitious and powerful vision for its customers.
truth_5.evidence: [PURPOSE] Both channels are 'The Empowerer,' but WWW promises 'Transformation' (Mission 5/5) while PDP promises 'Enhancement' (Mission 4/5). WWW: 'discover a better version of yourself'. PDP: 'firmer, bouncier, and more radiant complexion.'
truth_5.why_matters: This gap shows the brand is being too conservative on its highest-traffic channel. The bold, transformative mission on the website is the story that builds loyalty and justifies a premium price. By watering it down to simple 'enhancement' on Amazon, you are leaving brand equity and emotional connection on the table.
truth_5.the_hard_choice: Your website is selling a life-changing outcome, while your Amazon page is selling a slightly better photo. The hard choice is whether to trust your own boldest vision. Option A: Keep the conservative 'enhancement' messaging on Amazon to play it safe and focus on simple benefits. Option B: Port the powerful 'Transformation' narrative from the website to the PDP, elevating the conversation from 'radiant complexion' to 'renewed confidence.' The market has proven it responds to the product; now is the time to test if it will fall in love with the purpose.

marker_1.so_what: A strong but slightly diluted purpose. The core mission to empower is clear, but the intensity of that promise wavers between channels.

marker_2.so_what: A fractured vision. The brand is a tech leader ('Scientific Vanguard') on WWW but a mass-market player ('The Democratizers') on PDP, creating confusion about its ambition.

marker_3.so_what: Contradictory values. The brand values high-performance data ('Radical Transparency') on its site but prioritizes gentleness ('Safety First') on Amazon, sending mixed signals about its core principles.

marker_4.so_what: A severe positioning crisis. The brand is a premium 'Scientific Miracle' on WWW and an affordable 'Clean Essentialist' on PDP, directly eroding its pricing power.

marker_5.so_what: A complete personality split. The brand is a distant 'Reliable Expert' on its site and a warm 'Trendsetter' on Amazon, making it impossible to form a coherent relationship with the customer.

marker_6.so_what: Total identity confusion. The Sage/Ruler on WWW projects authority and wisdom, while the Caregiver/Lover on PDP projects nurturing and sensuality, creating two completely different brand souls.

marker_7.so_what: A jarring tonal shift. The voice moves from a 'Clinical Authority' on the website to a 'Gentle Guide' on the PDP, undermining its expert credibility.

marker_8.so_what: The brand's only point of true consistency. The 'Earthy Organic' visual system is a powerful, unifying asset that provides a stable foundation for the brand.

marker_9.so_what: The core message is inconsistent. WWW is a rational 'Problem Solver' using data, while PDP is an aspirational 'Promise Maker' using emotional language, confusing what the customer is buying.

marker_10.so_what: A mostly consistent origin story. Both channels tell the story of a 'Lab Breakthrough,' but with minor variations in the protagonist's authority.

marker_11.so_what: The reason to believe is broken. WWW proves its claims with clinical data ('Derm-Validation'), while PDP proves them with 'free-from' lists ('Formula Purity'), appealing to two different value systems.

marker_12.so_what: The unique selling proposition is incoherent. The brand is a niche 'Specialist' on its site but a broader 'Tech Engineer' on Amazon, muddying its competitive advantage.

marker_13.so_what: A guarded but acceptable alignment. Both channels focus on 'Science Education,' but the PDP execution is less rigorous, creating a slight dilution of the educational mission.

marker_14.so_what: Strong functional alignment. Both channels clearly communicate the benefit of 'Correction' for aesthetic skin concerns, providing a rare point of clarity for the customer.

marker_15.so_what: A confused emotional promise. The brand offers 'Confidence' on its website but 'Intelligence' on Amazon, failing to establish a consistent emotional reward for the user.

marker_16.so_what: A conflicting identity signal. Using the brand makes you a 'Pragmatist' on WWW but a 'Purist' on PDP, appealing to customers with fundamentally different self-images.

marker_17.so_what: A total split in social currency. The brand offers inclusion in 'The Safe Choice' majority on its site, but entry into 'The Knowledge Club' niche on Amazon, fracturing any sense of community.
